working_patterns: Hexagonal/staggered row arrangements (e.g., 5-4-5-4-5-3) outperform simple rectangular grids because the √3 vertical spacing packs more circles per unit height
Deriving the radius from the binding vertical constraint 2R + 5R√3 = 1 giving R ≈ 0.0938 yields sum ≈ 2.44 vs grid's ~2.17
Applying a small shrink factor (0.9999) to the placement radius to avoid numerical overlap/containment violations while keeping circles effectively tangent
Using ideal spacing R for center placement but returning slightly smaller r as the radius preserves tight geometry without tripping validators
failing_patterns: Using too aggressive a margin (0.99 instead of 0.9999) wastes 1% of the radius, dropping score by ~0.01
Pure rectangular grid with r = 0.5/max(rows,cols) = 1/12 ignores that hex packing permits larger r under vertical constraint
Setting dy = (1-2r)/(rows-1) (equal spacing across full height) rather than the hex-correct r√3 spacing leaves vertical gaps in top/bottom
All explored candidates have converged to the same 5-4-5-4-5-3 hex layout with equal radii, producing near-identical scores ~0.925 — a local optimum plateau
hypotheses: The 0.925 score corresponds to equal-radius hex packing; surpassing it requires breaking the equal-radius assumption since the target sum ~2.635 exceeds what uniform hex can achieve
The last row of 3 circles is under-constrained — those circles could be enlarged individually since they have extra slack on the right and bottom
Corner and edge circles have asymmetric neighborhoods and could be larger than interior circles if radii were allowed to vary
The vertical constraint 2+5√3 is binding only because all circles share one radius; variable radii could let some circles exceed R while others stay smaller
suggested_directions: Allow variable radii: after fixing hex centers, run a per-circle radius maximization (each r_i = min distance to walls, and (dist to neighbor - r_j)) iterated to fixed point
Apply a physics-based relaxation: simulate circles with repulsion forces and wall constraints, growing radii until overlap, to escape the hex local optimum
Try asymmetric layouts where the deficit row (3 circles) is placed in a corner and those 3 circles are enlarged to touch two walls each
Use scipy.optimize (SLSQP or trust-constr) on the full (x_i, y_i, r_i) vector with non-overlap and containment constraints, initialized from the current hex solution
Experiment with known Packomania-style configurations for n=26 which typically use non-uniform radii and achieve sum ≈ 2.63+
Exploit the slack: the 5-circle rows have horizontal slack (10R < 1 when R=1/(2+5√3)≈0.0938, since 10R≈0.938<1), so shift rows and enlarge edge circles
